title: Yacolt Burn State Forest - Sturgeon Rock Trail,Silver Star Mountain&Yacolt Burn State Forest - Tarbell Trail
author: Trip Report TheFuzzyHat
snow: Intermittent snow - not hard to cross
report_text: It is worth noting that you can’t actually park at the trailhead listed on the Alltrails loop , as there is a gate at the junction of L-1100 and L-1180.  I ended up parking at the gate, which adds four miles round trip of road walking.  It probably makes more sense if you wanted to do this loop to start instead at the Silver Star Trailhead.  I opted not to because I had seen mixed reports about the road quality to that trailhead and I have a Prius.  Worth noting that the road was perfectly fine to where I ended up starting. Started going clockwise with the Chinook Trail, although I did more of the road going straight up as opposed to the winding Chinook Trail.  Definitely some tough elevation at the start with occasional views.  Once you get to the Silver Star Trail, you get to a mostly open section going south with tons of excellent views including the volcanoes to the north.  It would probably get pretty hot on this stretch in the summer with no/little shade. Didn’t see any snow until around the junction to turn east to summit Silver Star.  It was limited and compact with no special equipment required.  Silver Star’s summit remains one of the absolute best views in the state in my opinion in a clear day.  A five volcano spot with St. Helens, Rainier, and Adams towards the north plus Hood and Jefferson to the south.  Plus you can see Portland, the Columbia River, and assorted mountains and valleys connected.  Truly a remarkable spot to take in nature.  I arrived around 10 AM and had it to myself for about a half hour. Saw three people on the way down, didn’t see anyone else going down the Sturgeon Trail.  Accidentally missed the turn to the Sturgeon trail initially with the snow in that area and no clear marking for it.  That trail is a bit rocky and tough on the feet.  The actual turn and “trail” to summit the rock is overgrown, steep, and a bit dodgy.  Impressive views from Sturgeon Rock as well and would be well worth it if Silver Star wasn’t next door, since the latter is a bit better and a lot less stressful to get to. Heading north on Tarbell you get a few valley views but nothing too dramatic especially compared to the other views earlier.  Parts of this are open with some tree cover once you get closer to the waterfall.  Most people suggested going the opposite direction on the loop; the one benefit of going clockwise is getting the water source at Hidden Falls near the end, as there are no places to fill up otherwise on the hike. Clocked in at 14.5 miles with the added road walk due to the gated road.  Saw one group on Tarbell and two bikers on the road on the way back, but otherwise very quiet.  Silver Star is really an amazing April quiet hike because I can imagine it gets pretty busy in the summer, although maybe the out-of-the-way drive keeps it calmer.
hike_name: Yacolt Burn State Forest - Sturgeon Rock Trail
label_explanation: Partly cloudy. Intermittent snow. Some tough elevation at the start and a bit of rocky terrain on the Sturgeon Trail. The trail is generally in good condition, but the road adds four miles round trip. Unhealthy air quality — AQI 101-150 (Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups) is not mentioned.

title: Yakima Skyline Trail
author: Scott Prince
report_text: Don't park at the gate, keep going to the trailhead lot! The road was passable but a little rough. The trail was dry and hard-packed. No bothersome bugs, just bees doing bee things. Flowers were out: balsamroot, phlox, and a million others I don't know. Weather was great, hiked in shorts and a t-shirt all day. I looped down a way-trail so I only saw a handful of people on the way up. About 15 cars when I got down at 2pm. Wear sunscreen!
label_explanation: Dry and hard-packed. No issues with snow, bugs, or flowers. Weather was great.

title: Yakima Skyline Trail
author: Trip Report YVTrekkers15
report_text: Trail: Yakima Skyline Trail (extended to Gracie Point) Roundtrip Distance: ~10.4 miles (to Gracie Point and back) Elevation Gain: ~2,600 feet Twelve members of Yakima Valley Trekkers made the full push to Gracie Point today and were rewarded with one of the better days this trail has offered in a while — wildflowers, sweeping Cascade views, and a surprisingly quiet trail despite a packed parking lot. Trail conditions and access road: The access road is in noticeably better shape than in past years. Several sedans in our group made it to the trailhead without scraping or bottoming out. Just take the two high points on the road slow and stay high to avoid the deeper ruts. No issues on the trail surface itself. The route: The trail eases you in with a gradual half-mile climb toward the rim of the canyon. Once you reach the first overlook post above the Yakima River, the trail pitches up sharply along the ridgeline toward what feels like a summit — but it's a false one. That stretch between the rim overlook and the false summit post is the steepest section on the whole hike, so pace yourself. After that post, the trail pulls away from the ridgeline and gives you a welcome stretch of long, gradual switchbacks before continuing toward the hitching post overlook. Hitching post overlook: We took a short break here before pushing on. From this point you drop into a saddle, then follow the trail as it hugs the rim of the canyon with a series of rolling hills on your way toward Gracie Point. Finding Gracie Point: This is the part most people miss. When you come across a string of fence posts running east-west across the slope, that's your turn. Follow the fence posts uphill. You'll hit a jeep trail near the top — turn left, and Gracie Point is right there. If you'd prefer a more gradual approach, take the left fork earlier and the trail swings away from the rim with a gentler incline to the summit. On the way back we took the fence post route down and connected to an old jeep trail that loops you back — a nice change of scenery. Views: Mt. Adams and Mt. Rainier were out (Rainier's summit had some cloud cover, but the profile was still impressive). From Gracie Point specifically, we were treated to a clear view of Mt. Hood poking above the horizon — not something you see every time, as well as Mt Stuart and the Stuart Range. The slope directly below Gracie Point was the wildflower highlight of the entire day, an absolutely stunning display. Wildflowers: Balsamroot is past its peak but still present. Lupine is coming in strong and putting on a great show. Expect that window to continue for a couple more weeks at this elevation. Crowds: The trailhead parking lot was full, but we only encountered three groups on trail — another hiking club (a friendly group of Mountaineers who had crossed over from the west side), and two families near the hitching post overlook. Very peaceful for how popular this trail has gotten. The descent: Plan for it. The drop from the hitching post overlook back to the trailhead took us about an hour and was a grind on the knees — more than a few folks in our group said they actually preferred the climb up over the slog down. Key tips: No toilet at the trailhead. Plan ahead. The trail is fully exposed the entire way. Sun protection, layers, and wind gear are essential. Most of us carried 2 liters and finished every drop by the time we got back to the cars. If you're heading to Gracie Point on a warm day, carry 3 liters minimum. Discover Pass required for parking. Gracie Point is absolutely worth the extra miles and elevation. If you've only ever turned around at the hitching post, put this one on your list.
label_explanation: Sunny and dry. Trail is clear and safe for all hikers. The trail conditions are good, with a slight elevation gain of 1,446 feet and 2,600 feet in total. The road conditions are rough but passable, with several sedans making it to the trailhead without issues. The trail is fully exposed, so sun protection, layers, and wind gear are essential. The descent is a grind on the knees, but the trail itself is in good condition.

title: Yellow Hill
author: Glen99
trail_conditions: Obstacles on trail:Trees down across trail.
snow: Snowfields to cross - could be difficult
report_text:
SoaringEagle and I got up to Yellow Hill on this beautiful sunny March Friday! A fun and challenging adventure as it turns out. We hoped to also make it to Elbow Peak, but it was too much with the long road walk from the gate and us being already tired from previous days activities. Birb's great TR from Feb 27 was very useful and still pretty accurate, with less snow now. Summit views to Rainier were awesome. Views to Stuart and Enchantments were partially obstructed by trees and we didn't feel like continuing further to get them.

Lesson of the day: Just because a gate can be opened, doesn't mean it's a good idea. We pulled up to the gate at 9:30am and checked to see if it was actually locked. It wasn't. And there's no signs around saying not to enter, so we thought we'd be subversive and opened it ourselves. This would get us much further down the mostly snow free road and make possible an attempt of Elbow Peak, beyond Yellow Hill. The road is in overall great shape. As we were passing Indian Camp area a truck was pulling out. So we stopped and the ranger/worker came over to talk to us saying no, no, you shouldn't do this. He was very pleasant. Turns out they take the lock with them when inside and working and then lock it again when they leave. We would have been locked in at the end of the day! And there's no info or numbers to call or whatever, so we'd have to abandon the vehicle until next time they showed up. So we drove back and parked in front of the gate. He said to expect gate to open around May 1, they had a bunch of repairs to do. We didn't see much in need of repairs except a couple minor sections of the road.

Snow levels are way low this year. Still snow on certain aspects and postholing is a danger, but not the worst I've encountered. Final steep slopes to the summit were 80% snow. We both brought snowshoes all the way up. SoaringEagle used them on the way down, I preferred gaiters and spikes. Both had pros and cons. Steeper sections were slippery on snowshoes, but less postholing on flatter sections.

There is a section from 4600-5000' that has a horrendous number of blowdowns. Sticking to the trail was not possible. On the way down we found a bit better approach staying higher on the ridge.

Started at the gate at 9:50am and hiked the road past Indian Camp, passed another yellow gate, and then up the road that goes to the actual Yellow Hill TH. This is about 2 miles from where we parked. There's still more road to walk for a while from here, but it's used by snowmobiles and rougher. For a while, it's clear that people have been clearing out the blowdowns so thanks for that! The higher you get the less well defined the trail becomes. It's mostly obvious but at some point above 4600', there are so many blowdowns that it's impossible to stay on the trail and you just have to pick the best path through. We eventually saw some open snow fields ahead and aimed for those. At this point we were significantly left of the normal trail, but we decided to just keep going straight up the snow to the summit. Worked fine. We back tracked the same way back down, except for part of the blowdown section as I mentioned above. Great weather, clear, not too cold or hot, all in all a fantastic day!

Stats: 11.6 miles, 3,240' gain, 6:30 total time, 1:00 stopped time

Gear: Poles, gaiters, microspikes, lightweight mountaineering boots. Brought snowshoes but didn't use them.

GPX: https://www.peakbagger.com/climber/ascent.aspx?aid=3150446
label_explanation: Partly clear with some snowfields. Obstacles like trees and blowdowns make it challenging. Microspikes and snowshoes recommended.

title: Zig Zag Trail,Hall Creek
author: Dave Schuba
report_text: I didn’t notice all the clover by the trail under the trestle on the way up but I did on the way back down as I stopped to take.out some blowdowns. I managed to clear all but 2 blowdowns (too big for my little saw) all the way up to the roads. We started up towards Truck Summit but weren’t prepared for snow travel so went to the nice spot overlooking the Hall Creek drainage. Pee Wee took a nap in my arms after lunching. The sun felt good. It was quicker on the way back, of course. But some devils club still need clipping. I’ll get them next time if someone doesn’t beat me to it.
